1	import type { BasicBuilder } from './BasicBuilder'
2	
3	export const INDENT_SIZE = 2
4	/**
5	 * Helper class for building long multi-line formatted strings from building blocks.
6	 * Can use either plain strings or `Builder` objects, that would encapsulate formatting logic.
7	 */
8	export class Writer<ContextType = undefined> {
9	  private lines: string[] = []
10	  private currentLine = ''
11	  private currentIndent = 0
12	  private marginSymbol?: string
13	  private afterNextNewLineCallback?: () => void
14	
15	  constructor(
16	    startingIndent = 0,
17	    readonly context: ContextType,
18	  ) {
19	    this.currentIndent = startingIndent
20	  }
21	
22	  /**
23	   * Adds provided value to the current line. Does not end the line.
24	   *
25	   * @param value
26	   * @returns
27	   */
28	  write(value: string | BasicBuilder<ContextType>): this {
29	    if (typeof value === 'string') {
30	      this.currentLine += value
31	    } else {
32	      value.write(this)
33	    }
34	    return this
35	  }
36	
37	  /**
38	   * Adds several `values` to the current line, separated by `separator`. Both values and separator
39	   * can also be `Builder` instances for more advanced formatting.
40	   *
41	   * @param separator
42	   * @param values
43	   * @param writeItem allow to customize how individual item is written
44	   * @returns
45	   */
46	  writeJoined<T extends string | BasicBuilder<ContextType>>(
47	    separator: string | BasicBuilder<ContextType>,
48	    values: T[],
49	    writeItem: (item: T, writer: this) => void = (item, w) => w.write(item),
50	  ): this {
51	    const last = values.length - 1
52	    for (let i = 0; i < values.length; i++) {
53	      writeItem(values[i], this)
54	      if (i !== last) {
55	        this.write(separator)
56	      }
57	    }
58	    return this
59	  }
60	
61	  /**
62	   * Adds a string to current line, flushes current line and starts a new line.
63	   * @param line
64	   * @returns
65	   */
66	  writeLine(line: string | BasicBuilder<ContextType>): this {
67	    return this.write(line).newLine()
68	  }
69	
70	  /**
71	   * Flushes current line and starts a new line. New line starts at previously configured indentation level
72	   * @returns
73	   */
74	  newLine(): this {
75	    this.lines.push(this.indentedCurrentLine())
76	    this.currentLine = ''
77	    this.marginSymbol = undefined
78	
79	    const afterNextNewLineCallback = this.afterNextNewLineCallback
80	    this.afterNextNewLineCallback = undefined
81	    afterNextNewLineCallback?.()
82	    return this
83	  }
84	
85	  /**
86	   * Increases indentation level by 1, calls provided callback and then decreases indentation again.
87	   * Could be used for writing indented blocks of text:
88	   *
89	   * @example
90	   * ```ts
91	   * writer
92	   *   .writeLine('{')
93	   *   .withIndent(() => {
94	   *      writer.writeLine('foo: 123');
95	   *      writer.writeLine('bar: 456');
96	   *   })
97	   *   .writeLine('}')
98	   * ```
99	   * @param callback
100	   * @returns
101	   */
102	  withIndent(callback: (writer: this) => void): this {
103	    this.indent()
104	    callback(this)
105	    this.unindent()
106	    return this
107	  }
108	
109	  /**
110	   * Calls provided callback next time when new line is started.
111	   * Callback is called after old line have already been flushed and a new
112	   * line have been started. Can be used for adding "between the lines" decorations,
113	   * such as underlines.
114	   *
115	   * @param callback
116	   * @returns
117	   */
118	  afterNextNewline(callback: () => void) {
119	    this.afterNextNewLineCallback = callback
120	    return this
121	  }
122	
123	  /**
124	   * Increases indentation level of the current line by 1
125	   * @returns
126	   */
127	  indent(): this {
128	    this.currentIndent++
129	    return this
130	  }
131	
132	  /**
133	   * Decreases indentation level of the current line by 1, if it is possible
134	   * @returns
135	   */
136	  unindent(): this {
137	    if (this.currentIndent > 0) {
138	      this.currentIndent--
139	    }
140	    return this
141	  }
142	
143	  /**
144	   * Adds a symbol, that will replace the first character of the current line (including indentation)
145	   * when it is flushed. Can be used for adding markers to the line.
146	   *
147	   * Note: if indentation level of the line is 0, it will replace the first actually printed character
148	   * of the line. Use with caution.
149	   * @param symbol
150	   * @returns
151	   */
152	  addMarginSymbol(symbol: string): this {
153	    this.marginSymbol = symbol
154	    return this
155	  }
156	
157	  toString() {
158	    return this.lines.concat(this.indentedCurrentLine()).join('\n')
159	  }
160	
161	  getCurrentLineLength() {
162	    return this.currentLine.length
163	  }
164	
165	  private indentedCurrentLine(): string {
166	    const line = this.currentLine.padStart(this.currentLine.length + INDENT_SIZE * this.currentIndent)
167	    if (this.marginSymbol) {
168	      return this.marginSymbol + line.slice(1)
169	    }
170	    return line
171	  }
172	}

6	import path from 'path'

8	import { debounce } from '../blaze/debounce'
9	import { flatten } from '../blaze/flatten'
10	import { handle } from '../blaze/handle'
11	import { map } from '../blaze/map'
12	import { omit } from '../blaze/omit'

20	export type BuildResult = esbuild.BuildResult
21	export type BuildOptions = esbuild.BuildOptions & {
22	  name?: string
23	  emitTypes?: boolean
24	  emitMetafile?: boolean
25	  outbase?: never // we don't support this
26	}
27	
28	const DEFAULT_BUILD_OPTIONS = {
29	  platform: 'node',
30	  target: 'ES2022',
31	  logLevel: 'error',
32	  tsconfig: 'tsconfig.build.json',
33	  metafile: true,
34	} as const
35	
36	/**
37	 * Apply defaults to the original build options
38	 * @param options the original build options
39	 */
40	const applyDefaults = (options: BuildOptions): BuildOptions => ({
41	  ...DEFAULT_BUILD_OPTIONS,
42	  format: 'cjs',
43	  outExtension: { '.js': '.js' },
44	  resolveExtensions: ['.ts', '.js', '.node'],
45	  entryPoints: glob.sync('./src/**/*.{j,t}s', {
46	    ignore: ['./src/__tests__/**/*'],
47	  }),
48	  mainFields: ['module', 'main'],
49	  ...options,
50	  // outfile has precedence over outdir, hence these ternaries
51	  outfile: options.outfile ? getOutFile(options) : undefined,
52	  outdir: options.outfile ? undefined : getOutDir(options),
53	  plugins: [
54	    ...(options.plugins ?? []),
55	    resolvePathsPlugin,
56	    fixImportsPlugin,
57	    tscPlugin(options.emitTypes),
58	    onErrorPlugin,
59	  ],
60	  external: [...(options.external ?? []), ...getProjectExternals(options)],
61	})
62	
63	/**
64	 * Create two deferred builds for esm and cjs. The one follows the other:
65	 * - 1. The code gets compiled to an optimized tree-shaken esm output
66	 * - 2. We take that output and compile it to an optimized cjs output
67	 * @param options the original build options
68	 * @returns if options = [a, b], we get [a-esm, a-cjs, b-esm, b-cjs]
69	 */
70	function createBuildOptions(options: BuildOptions[]) {
71	  return flatten(
72	    map(options, (options) => [
73	      // we defer it so that we don't trigger glob immediately
74	      () => applyDefaults(options),
75	      // ... here can go more steps
76	    ]),
77	  )
78	}
79	
80	/**
81	 * We only want to trigger the glob search once we are ready, and that is when
82	 * the previous build has finished. We get the build options from the deferred.
83	 */
84	function computeOptions(options: () => BuildOptions) {
85	  return options()
86	}
87	
88	/**
89	 * Extensions are not automatically by esbuild set for `options.outfile`. We
90	 * look at the set `options.outExtension` and we add that to `options.outfile`.
91	 */
92	function addExtensionFormat(options: BuildOptions) {
93	  if (options.outfile && options.outExtension) {
94	    const ext = options.outExtension['.js']
95	
96	    options.outfile = `${options.outfile}${ext}`
97	  }
98	
99	  return options
100	}
101	
102	/**
103	 * If we don't have `options.outfile`, we default `options.outdir`
104	 */
105	function addDefaultOutDir(options: BuildOptions) {
106	  if (options.outfile === undefined) {
107	    options.outdir = getOutDir(options)
108	  }
109	
110	  return options
111	}
112	
113	/**
114	 * Execute esbuild with all the configurations we pass
115	 */
116	async function executeEsBuild(options: BuildOptions) {
117	  if (process.env.WATCH === 'true') {
118	    const context = await esbuild.context(omit(options, ['name', 'emitTypes', 'emitMetafile']) as any)
119	
120	    watch(context, options)
121	  }
122	
123	  const build = await esbuild.build(omit(options, ['name', 'emitTypes', 'emitMetafile']) as any)
124	  const outdir = options.outdir ?? (options.outfile ? path.dirname(options.outfile) : undefined)
125	
126	  if (build.metafile && options.emitMetafile) {
127	    const metafilePath = `${outdir}/${options.name}.meta.json`
128	    writeFileSync(metafilePath, JSON.stringify(build.metafile))
129	  }
130	
131	  return [options, build] as const
132	}
133	
134	/**
135	 * Execution pipeline that applies a set of actions
136	 * @param options
137	 */
138	export async function build(options: BuildOptions[]) {
139	  return transduce.async(
140	    createBuildOptions(options),
141	    pipe.async(computeOptions, logStartBuild, addExtensionFormat, addDefaultOutDir, executeEsBuild),
142	  )
143	}
144	
145	/**
146	 * Prints a message every time a new bundle is built

168	
169	  // triggers quick rebuild on file change

171	    const timeBefore = Date.now()
172	
173	    // we handle possible rebuild exceptions
174	    const rebuildResult = await handle.async(() => {
175	      return context.rebuild()
176	    })
177	
178	    // Handle unexpected internal errors
179	    if (rebuildResult instanceof Error) {
180	      console.error(rebuildResult)
181	      // Handle build errors (e.g., syntax errors)
182	    } else if (rebuildResult.errors.length > 0) {
183	      // Log the detailed error object from esbuild for better debugging.
184	      console.error(rebuildResult.errors)
185	    }
186	
187	    console.log(`${Date.now() - timeBefore}ms [${options.name ?? ''}]`)
188	  }, 10)
189	
190	  changeWatcher.on('change', fastRebuild)
191	
192	  return undefined
193	}
194	
195	// Utils ::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
196	
197	// get a default directory if needed (no outfile)
198	function getOutDir(options: BuildOptions) {
199	  if (options.outfile !== undefined) {
200	    return path.dirname(options.outfile)
201	  }
202	
203	  return options.outdir ?? 'dist'
204	}
205	
206	// get the output file from an original path
207	function getOutFile(options: BuildOptions) {
208	  if (options.outfile !== undefined) {
209	    const dirname = getOutDir(options)
210	    const filename = path.basename(options.outfile)
211	
212	    return `${dirname}/${filename}`
213	  }
214	
215	  return undefined
216	}
217	
218	// get the current project externals this helps to mark dependencies as external
219	// by having convention in the package.json (dev = bundled, non-dev = external)
220	function getProjectExternals(options: BuildOptions) {
221	  const pkg = require(`${process.cwd()}/package.json`)
222	  const peerDeps = Object.keys(pkg.peerDependencies ?? {})
223	  const regDeps = Object.keys(pkg.dependencies ?? {})
224	
225	  // when bundling, only the devDeps will be bundled
226	  if (!process.env.IGNORE_EXTERNALS && options.bundle === true) {
227	    return [...new Set([...peerDeps, ...regDeps])]
228	  }
229	
230	  // otherwise, all the dependencies will be bundled
231	  return []
232	}
233

I now have all the information needed. Here is the complete implementation plan.

---

# Implementation Plan: `@prisma/rest-client-generator`

## 1. Complete File List

### New files to create

```
packages/rest-client-generator/
  package.json
  tsconfig.json
  tsconfig.build.json
  helpers/
    build.ts
  src/
    index.ts
    generator.ts
    generate-client.ts
    type-map.ts
    model-file-generator.ts
    __tests__/
      type-map.test.ts
      model-file-generator.test.ts
```

### Existing files to modify

```
tsconfig.build.bundle.json    — add one entry to `paths`
```

---

## 2. Exact `package.json` Content

```json
{
  "name": "@prisma/rest-client-generator",
  "version": "0.0.0",
  "description": "This package is intended for Prisma's internal use",
  "main": "dist/index.js",
  "module": "dist/index.mjs",
  "types": "dist/index.d.ts",
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "require": {
        "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
        "default": "./dist/index.js"
      },
      "import": {
        "types": "./dist/index.d.mts",
        "default": "./dist/index.mjs"
      }
    }
  },
  "repository": {
    "type": "git",
    "url": "https://github.com/prisma/prisma.git",
    "directory": "packages/rest-client-generator"
  },
  "license": "Apache-2.0",
  "dependencies": {
    "@prisma/dmmf": "workspace:*",
    "@prisma/generator": "workspace:*",
    "@prisma/generator-helper": "workspace:*",
    "@prisma/internals": "workspace:*",
    "@prisma/ts-builders": "workspace:*"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "DEV=true tsx helpers/build.ts",
    "build": "tsx helpers/build.ts",
    "prepublishOnly": "pnpm run build",
    "test": "vitest run"
  },
  "files": [
    "dist"
  ],
  "sideEffects": false
}
```

**Dependency reasoning:**
- `@prisma/dmmf` — DMMF type definitions used throughout.
- `@prisma/generator` — `GeneratorConfig`, `GeneratorManifest`, `GeneratorOptions` types.
- `@prisma/generator-helper` — `generatorHandler` for the JSON-RPC stdin/stderr protocol.
- `@prisma/internals` — `parseEnvValue` to resolve the `output` EnvValue.
- `@prisma/ts-builders` — All code-generation builder primitives.

No extra runtime dependencies. Everything else (`fs/promises`, `path`) is Node built-ins. Unlike `client-generator-js`, this generator has no engine requirement, no WASM, no runtime copying — so it is dramatically simpler.

---

## 3. Exact `helpers/build.ts` Content

```typescript
import { build } from '../../../helpers/compile/build'
import { adapterConfig } from '../../../helpers/compile/configs'

void build(adapterConfig)
```

This is identical to `client-generator-js/helpers/build.ts` and `client-generator-ts/helpers/build.ts`. The `adapterConfig` export from `helpers/compile/configs.ts` is pre-configured for a single entry `src/index.ts` → `dist/index`, producing both CJS (`dist/index.js`) and ESM (`dist/index.mjs`) plus `.d.ts` / `.d.mts` declarations.

---

## 4. Exact `tsconfig.json` and `tsconfig.build.json` Content

`packages/rest-client-generator/tsconfig.json`:
```json
{
  "extends": "../../tsconfig.json",
  "include": ["./**/*"]
}
```

`packages/rest-client-generator/tsconfig.build.json`:
```json
{
  "extends": "../../tsconfig.build.regular.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src"
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}
```

Both are byte-for-byte identical to the same files in `client-generator-js` with only the package directory differing — the extends paths are relative and work the same.

---

## 5. Modification to `tsconfig.build.bundle.json`

Inside the `"paths"` object, append one entry at the end (before the closing `}` of `paths`). The alphabetical convention is not strictly enforced but inserting after `@prisma/query-plan-executor` and before the existing `@prisma/sqlcommenter*` entries is a clean spot:

```json
"@prisma/rest-client-generator": ["./packages/rest-client-generator/src"],
```

Full location in file: after line 38 (`"@prisma/query-plan-executor"`), before line 39 (`"@prisma/sqlcommenter"`).

---

## 6. Detailed Source File Designs

### 6.1 `src/type-map.ts`

**Purpose.** Map a DMMF scalar `type` string to a `@prisma/ts-builders` `TypeBuilder` instance. This is a pure lookup — no I/O, no side effects.

DMMF scalar types and their TypeScript mappings:

| DMMF `field.type` | TypeScript type | Builder |
|---|---|---|
| `String` | `string` | `stringType` |
| `Boolean` | `boolean` | `booleanType` |
| `Int` | `number` | `numberType` |
| `Float` | `number` | `numberType` |
| `Decimal` | `string` | `stringType` (serialised as string over JSON) |
| `BigInt` | `string` | `stringType` (BigInt is not JSON-native) |
| `DateTime` | `string` | `stringType` (ISO-8601 over REST) |
| `Json` | `unknown` | `unknownType` |
| `Bytes` | `string` | `stringType` (base64 over REST) |

Any unknown scalar falls back to `unknownType`.

```typescript
import { TypeBuilder, booleanType, numberType, stringType, unknownType } from '@prisma/ts-builders'

const SCALAR_MAP: Record<string, TypeBuilder> = {
  String: stringType,
  Boolean: booleanType,
  Int: numberType,
  Float: numberType,
  Decimal: stringType,
  BigInt: stringType,
  DateTime: stringType,
  Json: unknownType,
  Bytes: stringType,
}

export function scalarToTypeBuilder(dmmfType: string): TypeBuilder {
  return SCALAR_MAP[dmmfType] ?? unknownType
}
```

### 6.2 `src/model-file-generator.ts`

**Purpose.** Given one `DMMF.Model`, the complete set of `DMMF.DatamodelEnum` (for enum type resolution), and the model name, produce all the `TypeBuilder` / `BasicBuilder` nodes that will appear in the output file for that model. Returns them as an array of `FileItem` (from `@prisma/ts-builders`'s `File` type).

**Imports used from `@prisma/ts-builders`:**
`interfaceDeclaration`, `typeDeclaration`, `moduleExport`, `property`, `method`, `parameter`, `namedType`, `array`, `unionType`, `stringType`, `numberType`, `booleanType`, `unknownType`, `functionType`, `stringify`

**REST path convention.** Convert the model name from PascalCase to lowercase kebab-case plural:
- `User` → `/users`
- `BlogPost` → `/blog-posts`

Implementation: insert a hyphen before each uppercase letter that follows a lowercase letter or digit (`/(?<=[a-z0-9])(?=[A-Z])/g`), convert to lowercase, and append an `s`. This is a simple convention; no external pluralisation library is needed.

**Field filtering rules** (see section 7 for full detail). The generator function per model produces, in order:

1. `${ModelName}` interface (base)
2. `${ModelName}CreateInput` type
3. `${ModelName}UpdateInput` type
4. `${ModelName}WhereUniqueInput` type
5. `${ModelName}WhereInput` type
6. `${ModelName}Client` interface
7. `create${ModelName}Client` factory function type (as a `FunctionType` stored in a `constDeclaration`)

Signatures:

```typescript
import type * as DMMF from '@prisma/dmmf'
import { FileItem } from '@prisma/ts-builders'

export function generateModelDeclarations(
  model: DMMF.Model,
  enumNames: ReadonlySet<string>,
): FileItem[]
```

`enumNames` is used to distinguish enum fields from scalar fields when calling `scalarToTypeBuilder`.

**Return type for async methods.** Every method on `${ModelName}Client` returns `Promise<T>`. Build this with `namedType('Promise').addGenericArgument(T)`.

### 6.3 `src/generate-client.ts`

**Purpose.** Orchestrate building the full output `index.ts` string and write it to disk.

```typescript
import fs from 'node:fs/promises'
import path from 'node:path'

import type * as DMMF from '@prisma/dmmf'
import { file, moduleExport, stringify, typeDeclaration, ... } from '@prisma/ts-builders'

export async function generateRestClient(
  dmmf: DMMF.Document,
  outputDir: string,
): Promise<void>
```

Steps inside:
1. `await fs.mkdir(outputDir, { recursive: true })` to ensure the directory exists.
2. Build enum union type declarations from `dmmf.datamodel.enums`.
3. Collect `enumNames = new Set(dmmf.datamodel.enums.map(e => e.name))`.
4. For each model in `dmmf.datamodel.models`, call `generateModelDeclarations(model, enumNames)`.
5. Assemble the aggregate `PrismaRestClient` interface and `createPrismaRestClient` factory (see section 6.4).
6. Wrap all declarations with `moduleExport(...)` and feed into a `file()` builder, then `stringify(fileBuilder)`.
7. Write the result to `path.join(outputDir, 'index.ts')` with `fs.writeFile`.

### 6.4 Aggregate `PrismaRestClient`

After processing all models, produce:

```typescript
export interface PrismaRestClient {
  user: UserClient
  post: PostClient
  // ...one property per model, camelCase model name
}

export declare function createPrismaRestClient(baseUrl: string): PrismaRestClient
```

The `createPrismaRestClient` declaration is emitted as an `export declare function` (not implemented — consumers use it as a type-safe factory). Because `ts-builders` does not have a "declare function" builder directly, emit this as a raw string appended after `stringify(fileBuilder)`, OR use the `ConstDeclaration` builder with a `FunctionType` to produce `export const createPrismaRestClient: (baseUrl: string) => PrismaRestClient`. The latter is cleaner and uses existing builders.

Model name → property name: lowercase first character of PascalCase. `User` → `user`, `BlogPost` → `blogPost`.

### 6.5 `src/generator.ts`

```typescript
import type { GeneratorConfig, GeneratorManifest, GeneratorOptions } from '@prisma/generator'
import { parseEnvValue } from '@prisma/internals'

import { generateRestClient } from './generate-client'

export class RestClientGenerator {
  readonly name = 'rest-client-generator'

  getManifest(_config: GeneratorConfig): Promise<GeneratorManifest> {
    return Promise.resolve({
      prettyName: 'Prisma REST Client',
      defaultOutput: 'generated/rest-client',
    })
  }

  async generate(options: GeneratorOptions): Promise<void> {
    const outputDir = parseEnvValue(options.generator.output!)
    await generateRestClient(options.dmmf, outputDir)
  }
}
```

The `defaultOutput` value `'generated/rest-client'` is relative to the schema directory and is the fallback when the user omits `output` in the schema. No engine or engine version is required — the `requiresEngines` list stays absent/empty.

### 6.6 `src/index.ts`

This is the executable entry point. `generatorHandler` from `@prisma/generator-helper` reads JSON-RPC from stdin and writes results to stderr.

```typescript
import { generatorHandler } from '@prisma/generator-helper'

import { RestClientGenerator } from './generator'

const generator = new RestClientGenerator()

generatorHandler({
  onManifest(config) {
    return generator.getManifest(config)
  },
  async onGenerate(options) {
    await generator.generate(options)
  },
})
```

The `generatorHandler` call blocks the process — it installs readline listeners on `process.stdin` and calls `process.stdin.resume()`. The process stays alive until Prisma closes the pipe.

---

## 7. Field Filtering Rules

### 7.1 Base interface `${ModelName}`

Include: every field where `kind === 'scalar'` or `kind === 'enum'`.

Exclude: fields where `kind === 'object'` (relations) or `kind === 'unsupported'`.

Type resolution:
- `kind === 'scalar'`: call `scalarToTypeBuilder(field.type)`.
- `kind === 'enum'`: `namedType(field.type)` — the enum union type is declared in the same file.

Optionality: if `!field.isRequired`, call `.optional()` on the property.

List: if `field.isList`, wrap the type with `array(...)`.

### 7.2 `${ModelName}CreateInput`

Start from the same scalar+enum fields as the base interface. Exclude a field from `CreateInput` when ANY of these is true:

- `field.isReadOnly === true` — database-computed columns.
- `field.isGenerated === true` — auto-generated (e.g. `@default(autoincrement())`).
- `field.isUpdatedAt === true` — maintained by Prisma (`@updatedAt`).
- `field.isId && field.hasDefaultValue` — auto-generated IDs (uuid, cuid, autoincrement). The combination of `isId` and `hasDefaultValue` indicates the client never sets this.

Fields that remain: mark as optional when `!field.isRequired || field.hasDefaultValue`.

Rationale: if a field has a default value or is not required, the REST client may omit it.

### 7.3 `${ModelName}UpdateInput`

All scalar+enum fields, all optional. Partial update semantics — every field is `?: T | undefined`.

Exclude: same exclusions as `CreateInput` (read-only, generated, updatedAt). ID fields are excluded from `UpdateInput` entirely (you don't update the identifier via the body).

### 7.4 `${ModelName}WhereUniqueInput`

An object with exactly the unique identifier fields: those where `field.isId === true` OR `field.isUnique === true`.

All properties are optional at the TypeScript level (the caller picks one), though at runtime exactly one must be supplied. All types come from the scalar map.

### 7.5 `${ModelName}WhereInput`

All scalar+enum fields, all optional. Used for filtering in list operations. Same type resolution as the base interface, but every property is optional.

---

## 8. Enum Handling

From `dmmf.datamodel.enums` (type `DMMF.DatamodelEnum[]`), each enum becomes:

```typescript
export type UserRole = 'ADMIN' | 'USER' | 'GUEST'
```

Implementation with `ts-builders`:

```typescript
import { moduleExport, stringLiteral, typeDeclaration, unionType } from '@prisma/ts-builders'

function enumDeclaration(e: DMMF.DatamodelEnum): Export<TypeDeclaration> {
  const variants = e.values.map((v) => stringLiteral(v.name))
  const union = unionType(variants)
  return moduleExport(typeDeclaration(e.name, union))
}
```

`stringLiteral(v.name)` produces `'ADMIN'`, `'USER'`, etc. The `unionType([...])` constructor accepts an array and produces `'ADMIN' | 'USER' | 'GUEST'`.

---

## 9. URL/Endpoint Convention

Convert the PascalCase model name to a lowercase kebab-case plural path segment.

```
User      → /users
Post      → /posts
BlogPost  → /blog-posts
UserRole  → /user-roles
```

Algorithm:
1. Insert `-` before every uppercase letter that is immediately preceded by a lowercase letter or digit: `/([a-z0-9])([A-Z])/g` → `$1-$2`.
2. Convert to lowercase.
3. Append `s` for plural.

This is implemented in a `toRestPath(modelName: string): string` pure function in `model-file-generator.ts`.

The 5 CRUD methods on `${ModelName}Client` use these endpoints:

| Method | Signature | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| `findMany` | `(where?: UserWhereInput) => Promise<User[]>` | `GET /users?where=...` |
| `findUnique` | `(where: UserWhereUniqueInput) => Promise<User \| null>` | `GET /users/:id` |
| `create` | `(data: UserCreateInput) => Promise<User>` | `POST /users` |
| `update` | `(where: UserWhereUniqueInput, data: UserUpdateInput) => Promise<User>` | `PATCH /users/:id` |
| `delete` | `(where: UserWhereUniqueInput) => Promise<User>` | `DELETE /users/:id` |

The `${ModelName}Client` interface is declared using `interfaceDeclaration(name).add(method(...))`. The `method(...)` builder takes the method name and uses `.setReturnType(...)` and `.addParameter(...)`. Return types are `Promise<T>` constructed as `namedType('Promise').addGenericArgument(namedType(ModelName))`, etc.

The factory function `create${ModelName}Client(baseUrl: string): ${ModelName}Client` has a concrete implementation using `fetch`. Since `ts-builders` operates on type-level constructs only, the factory body is emitted as a raw string appended to the file output after stringifying the structural declarations. This is the one place where raw string generation is used; everything else goes through the builder API.

The factory function body pattern:

```typescript
export function createUserClient(baseUrl: string): UserClient {
  const base = `${baseUrl}/users`
  return {
    async findMany(where) {
      const url = where ? `${base}?where=${encodeURIComponent(JSON.stringify(where))}` : base
      const res = await fetch(url)
      return res.json()
    },
    async findUnique(where) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`)
      if (res.status === 404) return null
      return res.json()
    },
    async create(data) {
      const res = await fetch(base, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify(data) })
      return res.json()
    },
    async update(where, data) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`, { method: 'PATCH', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify(data) })
      return res.json()
    },
    async delete(where) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`, { method: 'DELETE' })
      return res.json()
    },
  }
}
```

**Implementation strategy for the factory body.** In `generate-client.ts`, after calling `stringify(fileBuilder)` for all the interface/type declarations, concatenate the factory function strings separately. Each factory string is built in `model-file-generator.ts` by a second function `generateModelFactory(model: DMMF.Model): string` that returns the raw TypeScript source. The two strings are joined with `\n\n`.

---

## 10. Sample Generated Output

For the example schema model:

```prisma
enum UserRole { ADMIN USER }

model User {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  name      String
  email     String   @unique
  role      UserRole
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  posts     Post[]
}
```

The generated `index.ts` content for the `User` model section:

```typescript
export type UserRole = 'ADMIN' | 'USER'

export interface User {
  id: number
  name: string
  email: string
  role: UserRole
  createdAt: string
}

export type UserCreateInput = {
  name: string
  email: string
  role: UserRole
  createdAt?: string
}

export type UserUpdateInput = {
  name?: string
  email?: string
  role?: UserRole
  createdAt?: string
}

export type UserWhereUniqueInput = {
  id?: number
  email?: string
}

export type UserWhereInput = {
  id?: number
  name?: string
  email?: string
  role?: UserRole
  createdAt?: string
}

export interface UserClient {
  findMany(where?: UserWhereInput): Promise<User[]>
  findUnique(where: UserWhereUniqueInput): Promise<User | null>
  create(data: UserCreateInput): Promise<User>
  update(where: UserWhereUniqueInput, data: UserUpdateInput): Promise<User>
  delete(where: UserWhereUniqueInput): Promise<User>
}

export function createUserClient(baseUrl: string): UserClient {
  const base = `${baseUrl}/users`
  return {
    async findMany(where) {
      const url = where ? `${base}?where=${encodeURIComponent(JSON.stringify(where))}` : base
      const res = await fetch(url)
      return res.json()
    },
    async findUnique(where) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`)
      if (res.status === 404) return null
      return res.json()
    },
    async create(data) {
      const res = await fetch(base, {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify(data),
      })
      return res.json()
    },
    async update(where, data) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`, {
        method: 'PATCH',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify(data),
      })
      return res.json()
    },
    async delete(where) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`, { method: 'DELETE' })
      return res.json()
    },
  }
}
```

Field-by-field analysis for `User`:

- `id: Int @id @default(autoincrement())`: excluded from `CreateInput` and `UpdateInput` (is ID + has default). Present in `WhereUniqueInput` and `WhereInput`. Type: `number`.
- `name: String`: required, no default → required in `CreateInput`, optional in `UpdateInput`.
- `email: String @unique`: required, no default, unique → required in `CreateInput`, appears in `WhereUniqueInput`.
- `role: UserRole`: enum field, required → `UserRole` type in all input types.
- `createdAt: DateTime @default(now())`: has default → optional in `CreateInput` (written as `createdAt?: string`). Excluded from `UpdateInput` only if marked `@updatedAt`; since it is not `@updatedAt` in this schema, it IS included as optional in `UpdateInput`.
- `posts: Post[]`: `kind === 'object'` → excluded from all interfaces.

---

## 11. Verification Steps

### Step 1: Build the package

```sh
cd packages/rest-client-generator
pnpm build
```

Expected: `dist/index.js`, `dist/index.mjs`, `dist/index.d.ts`, `dist/index.d.mts` all created.

### Step 2: Run unit tests

```sh
cd packages/rest-client-generator
pnpm test
```

Tests in `src/__tests__/type-map.test.ts` should cover every DMMF scalar string and verify the output `TypeBuilder` name via `stringify`. Tests in `src/__tests__/model-file-generator.test.ts` should use a hand-crafted `DMMF.Model` object and verify the full generated output with `toMatchInlineSnapshot`.

### Step 3: Type-check with the monorepo tsconfig

```sh
# from repo root
pnpm tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.build.bundle.json
```

The new path alias entry means `@prisma/rest-client-generator` resolves to `./packages/rest-client-generator/src` for any other package that imports it.

### Step 4: End-to-end integration test

Create a minimal test fixture in `sandbox/` (already gitignored per the root `.gitignore` pattern):

```
sandbox/rest-test/
  schema.prisma
```

`schema.prisma`:
```prisma
generator restClient {
  provider = "node dist/index.js"
  output   = "./generated"
}

datasource db {
  provider = "sqlite"
  url      = "file:./dev.db"
}

model User {
  id    Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  name  String
  email String @unique
}
```

Run:
```sh
cd sandbox/rest-test
npx prisma generate
```

Expected: `sandbox/rest-test/generated/index.ts` is created with correct content.

Inspect:
```sh
cat sandbox/rest-test/generated/index.ts
```

### Step 5: Type-check the generated file

```sh
cd sandbox/rest-test
npx tsc --noEmit --strict generated/index.ts --target ES2022 --lib ES2022,DOM --moduleResolution bundler --module preserve
```

No errors expected.

### Step 6: Turbo build integration

From the repo root:
```sh
turbo build --filter=@prisma/rest-client-generator
```

Turbo reads `turbo.json`, sees the `build` task, and respects `dependsOn: ["^build"]`. The package's dependencies (`@prisma/dmmf`, `@prisma/generator`, `@prisma/generator-helper`, `@prisma/internals`, `@prisma/ts-builders`) must build first.

---

## 12. Architecture Notes and Trade-offs

**Why `generatorHandler` and not the `Generator` interface directly.** `client-generator-js` and `client-generator-ts` implement the `Generator` interface from `@prisma/generator` and are invoked programmatically through the `client-generator-registry`. An external generator (invoked via `provider = "node dist/index.js"`) must use the JSON-RPC protocol, which `generatorHandler` from `@prisma/generator-helper` implements. This package is designed as a standalone executable generator, not an internal programmatic one, so `generatorHandler` is the right primitive.

**Why raw string for factory bodies.** The `ts-builders` library produces typed declaration trees (interfaces, type aliases, methods). It has no `FunctionDeclaration` builder with a body. Emitting the factory function as a template-literal string is the pragmatic and convention-consistent approach — the declaration half (interface and types) uses builders, the implementation half uses raw strings. Both are joined in the final output with a blank line separator.

**Why `File` builder for declarations.** The `File` builder from `@prisma/ts-builders` handles proper newline spacing between declarations automatically (`\n` between items). Using it for the structural declarations avoids manual newline management.

**BigInt / Decimal as `string`.** REST/JSON cannot represent `BigInt` or `Decimal` natively. Serialising as `string` is the safest cross-platform convention. If callers need numeric precision, they parse the string themselves.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/packages/generator-helper/src/generatorHandler.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/packages/ts-builders/src/index.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/packages/dmmf/src/dmmf.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/helpers/compile/configs.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/tsconfig.build.bundle.json`

I need a detailed implementation plan for a new Prisma generator package called `@prisma/rest-client-generator`. This is a monorepo (pnpm workspaces + Turborepo) for Prisma ORM.

## Goal
The generator reads a Prisma schema's DMMF and produces a single TypeScript file (`index.ts`) in the user-specified output directory containing:
1. Union type aliases for every enum in the schema
2. TypeScript interfaces for every model (scalar + enum fields only; relation fields excluded)
3. Input types per model: `CreateInput`, `UpdateInput`, `WhereUniqueInput`, `WhereInput`
4. A REST client interface per model with 5 CRUD methods
5. A factory function per model using the native `fetch` API
6. An aggregate `PrismaRestClient` interface and `createPrismaRestClient` factory

## What I know about the monorepo (from code exploration)

### Generator Protocol (packages/generator-helper/src/generatorHandler.ts)
```typescript
export interface Handler {
  onGenerate(options: GeneratorOptions): Promise<any>
  onManifest?(config: GeneratorConfig): GeneratorManifest | Promise<GeneratorManifest>
}
export function generatorHandler(handler: Handler): void // reads stdin, writes JSON-RPC to stderr
```

### GeneratorOptions (packages/generator/src/configuration.ts)
```typescript
export type GeneratorOptions = {
  generator: GeneratorConfig   // has: name, output (EnvValue), config (Record<string,string>), sourceFilePath
  dmmf: DMMF.Document          // has: datamodel.models, datamodel.enums, datamodel.types
  schemaPath: string
  datasources: DataSource[]
  datamodel: string
  version: string
  // ...
}
export type GeneratorManifest = {
  prettyName?: string
  defaultOutput?: string
  denylists?: { models?: string[]; fields?: string[] }
  requiresGenerators?: string[]
  requiresEngines?: EngineType[]
  version?: string
  requiresEngineVersion?: string
}
```

### DMMF Types (packages/dmmf/src/dmmf.ts)
```typescript
type Model = { name: string; dbName: string | null; fields: Field[]; documentation?: string; ... }
type Field = {
  kind: 'scalar' | 'object' | 'enum' | 'unsupported'
  name: string
  isRequired: boolean   // false = optional (can be null)
  isList: boolean
  isUnique: boolean
  isId: boolean
  isReadOnly: boolean
  isGenerated?: boolean
  isUpdatedAt?: boolean
  hasDefaultValue: boolean
  type: string   // 'String','Int','Float','BigInt','Decimal','Boolean','DateTime','Json','Bytes',EnumName,ModelName
  documentation?: string
}
type DatamodelEnum = { name: string; values: EnumValue[]; documentation?: string }
type EnumValue = { name: string; dbName: string | null }
```

### ts-builders (packages/ts-builders/src/)
Key exports:
- `interfaceDeclaration(name)` → `.add(property(...))` → interface body
- `typeDeclaration(name, type)` → `export type Foo = ...`
- `property(name, type)` → `.optional()` for optional fields
- `method(name)` → `.setReturnType(type).addParameter(parameter(name, type))`
- `namedType(name)` → `Foo` or `Foo<Bar>`
- `objectType()` → `.add(property(...))` for inline object types
- `unionType([...])` → `A | B | C`
- `arrayType(elementType)` → `Foo[]`
- `stringType`, `numberType`, `booleanType`, `bigintType`, `unknownType` → primitives
- `stringLiteral(value)` → `'value'`
- `moduleExport(declaration)` → `export interface/type ...`
- `stringify(builder)` → returns the TypeScript string

### Existing package structure (reference: packages/client-generator-js)
```
packages/client-generator-js/
├── package.json         (main, module, types, exports, dependencies, scripts)
├── tsconfig.json        (extends root)
├── tsconfig.build.json  (extends ../../tsconfig.build.regular.json, outDir: dist)
├── helpers/build.ts     (imports adapterConfig from helpers/compile/configs.ts)
└── src/
    ├── index.ts
    ├── generator.ts
    └── generateClient.ts
```

`helpers/compile/configs.ts` exports `adapterConfig`:
- Single entry: `src/index.ts` → `dist/index`
- Produces both `dist/index.js` (CJS) and `dist/index.mjs` (ESM)
- Emits `.d.ts` and `.d.mts` type definitions

### tsconfig.build.bundle.json paths
Contains 42 path aliases. New entry `@prisma/rest-client-generator` must be added pointing to `./packages/rest-client-generator/src`.

### parseEnvValue (from @prisma/internals)
Used to resolve `output: EnvValue` from `generator.output` — same as other generators.

## What the plan should detail

Please provide a complete implementation plan that includes:

1. **Complete file list** with exact paths for every file to create/modify
2. **Exact package.json content** for the new package
3. **Exact helpers/build.ts content**
4. **Exact tsconfig.json and tsconfig.build.json content**
5. **Detailed source file designs** for each of:
   - `src/index.ts` — entry point, calls `generatorHandler()`
   - `src/generator.ts` — `RestClientGenerator` class with `getManifest`/`generate`
   - `src/generate-client.ts` — orchestrates generation, writes file with fs/promises
   - `src/type-map.ts` — DMMF scalar type string → TypeScript type builder function
   - `src/model-file-generator.ts` — generates all types and client code for one model

6. **Code generation design** showing exactly what TypeScript gets generated for a sample model like `User { id Int @id; name String; email String @unique; role UserRole; createdAt DateTime @default(now()); posts Post[] }`

7. **Field filtering rules**:
   - Which fields go in the base interface
   - Which are excluded from CreateInput (auto-generated IDs, computed fields)
   - UpdateInput rules
   - WhereUniqueInput rules  
   - WhereInput rules

8. **Enum handling**: generate union types from `DMMF.DatamodelEnum`

9. **URL/endpoint convention**: how model names map to REST paths

10. **The modification to tsconfig.build.bundle.json**

11. **Verification steps**: how to test end-to-end

Please write a complete, actionable plan. This is a monorepo so all paths should be relative to the repo root. The coding conventions are: kebab-case filenames, no barrel files (import from source directly), `#field` for private properties, no useless comments.

I now have all the information needed. Here is the complete implementation plan.

---

# Implementation Plan: `@prisma/rest-client-generator`

## 1. Complete File List

### New files to create

```
packages/rest-client-generator/
  package.json
  tsconfig.json
  tsconfig.build.json
  helpers/
    build.ts
  src/
    index.ts
    generator.ts
    generate-client.ts
    type-map.ts
    model-file-generator.ts
    __tests__/
      type-map.test.ts
      model-file-generator.test.ts
```

### Existing files to modify

```
tsconfig.build.bundle.json    — add one entry to `paths`
```

---

## 2. Exact `package.json` Content

```json
{
  "name": "@prisma/rest-client-generator",
  "version": "0.0.0",
  "description": "This package is intended for Prisma's internal use",
  "main": "dist/index.js",
  "module": "dist/index.mjs",
  "types": "dist/index.d.ts",
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "require": {
        "types": "./dist/index.d.ts",
        "default": "./dist/index.js"
      },
      "import": {
        "types": "./dist/index.d.mts",
        "default": "./dist/index.mjs"
      }
    }
  },
  "repository": {
    "type": "git",
    "url": "https://github.com/prisma/prisma.git",
    "directory": "packages/rest-client-generator"
  },
  "license": "Apache-2.0",
  "dependencies": {
    "@prisma/dmmf": "workspace:*",
    "@prisma/generator": "workspace:*",
    "@prisma/generator-helper": "workspace:*",
    "@prisma/internals": "workspace:*",
    "@prisma/ts-builders": "workspace:*"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "DEV=true tsx helpers/build.ts",
    "build": "tsx helpers/build.ts",
    "prepublishOnly": "pnpm run build",
    "test": "vitest run"
  },
  "files": [
    "dist"
  ],
  "sideEffects": false
}
```

**Dependency reasoning:**
- `@prisma/dmmf` — DMMF type definitions used throughout.
- `@prisma/generator` — `GeneratorConfig`, `GeneratorManifest`, `GeneratorOptions` types.
- `@prisma/generator-helper` — `generatorHandler` for the JSON-RPC stdin/stderr protocol.
- `@prisma/internals` — `parseEnvValue` to resolve the `output` EnvValue.
- `@prisma/ts-builders` — All code-generation builder primitives.

No extra runtime dependencies. Everything else (`fs/promises`, `path`) is Node built-ins. Unlike `client-generator-js`, this generator has no engine requirement, no WASM, no runtime copying — so it is dramatically simpler.

---

## 3. Exact `helpers/build.ts` Content

```typescript
import { build } from '../../../helpers/compile/build'
import { adapterConfig } from '../../../helpers/compile/configs'

void build(adapterConfig)
```

This is identical to `client-generator-js/helpers/build.ts` and `client-generator-ts/helpers/build.ts`. The `adapterConfig` export from `helpers/compile/configs.ts` is pre-configured for a single entry `src/index.ts` → `dist/index`, producing both CJS (`dist/index.js`) and ESM (`dist/index.mjs`) plus `.d.ts` / `.d.mts` declarations.

---

## 4. Exact `tsconfig.json` and `tsconfig.build.json` Content

`packages/rest-client-generator/tsconfig.json`:
```json
{
  "extends": "../../tsconfig.json",
  "include": ["./**/*"]
}
```

`packages/rest-client-generator/tsconfig.build.json`:
```json
{
  "extends": "../../tsconfig.build.regular.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src"
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}
```

Both are byte-for-byte identical to the same files in `client-generator-js` with only the package directory differing — the extends paths are relative and work the same.

---

## 5. Modification to `tsconfig.build.bundle.json`

Inside the `"paths"` object, append one entry at the end (before the closing `}` of `paths`). The alphabetical convention is not strictly enforced but inserting after `@prisma/query-plan-executor` and before the existing `@prisma/sqlcommenter*` entries is a clean spot:

```json
"@prisma/rest-client-generator": ["./packages/rest-client-generator/src"],
```

Full location in file: after line 38 (`"@prisma/query-plan-executor"`), before line 39 (`"@prisma/sqlcommenter"`).

---

## 6. Detailed Source File Designs

### 6.1 `src/type-map.ts`

**Purpose.** Map a DMMF scalar `type` string to a `@prisma/ts-builders` `TypeBuilder` instance. This is a pure lookup — no I/O, no side effects.

DMMF scalar types and their TypeScript mappings:

| DMMF `field.type` | TypeScript type | Builder |
|---|---|---|
| `String` | `string` | `stringType` |
| `Boolean` | `boolean` | `booleanType` |
| `Int` | `number` | `numberType` |
| `Float` | `number` | `numberType` |
| `Decimal` | `string` | `stringType` (serialised as string over JSON) |
| `BigInt` | `string` | `stringType` (BigInt is not JSON-native) |
| `DateTime` | `string` | `stringType` (ISO-8601 over REST) |
| `Json` | `unknown` | `unknownType` |
| `Bytes` | `string` | `stringType` (base64 over REST) |

Any unknown scalar falls back to `unknownType`.

```typescript
import { TypeBuilder, booleanType, numberType, stringType, unknownType } from '@prisma/ts-builders'

const SCALAR_MAP: Record<string, TypeBuilder> = {
  String: stringType,
  Boolean: booleanType,
  Int: numberType,
  Float: numberType,
  Decimal: stringType,
  BigInt: stringType,
  DateTime: stringType,
  Json: unknownType,
  Bytes: stringType,
}

export function scalarToTypeBuilder(dmmfType: string): TypeBuilder {
  return SCALAR_MAP[dmmfType] ?? unknownType
}
```

### 6.2 `src/model-file-generator.ts`

**Purpose.** Given one `DMMF.Model`, the complete set of `DMMF.DatamodelEnum` (for enum type resolution), and the model name, produce all the `TypeBuilder` / `BasicBuilder` nodes that will appear in the output file for that model. Returns them as an array of `FileItem` (from `@prisma/ts-builders`'s `File` type).

**Imports used from `@prisma/ts-builders`:**
`interfaceDeclaration`, `typeDeclaration`, `moduleExport`, `property`, `method`, `parameter`, `namedType`, `array`, `unionType`, `stringType`, `numberType`, `booleanType`, `unknownType`, `functionType`, `stringify`

**REST path convention.** Convert the model name from PascalCase to lowercase kebab-case plural:
- `User` → `/users`
- `BlogPost` → `/blog-posts`

Implementation: insert a hyphen before each uppercase letter that follows a lowercase letter or digit (`/(?<=[a-z0-9])(?=[A-Z])/g`), convert to lowercase, and append an `s`. This is a simple convention; no external pluralisation library is needed.

**Field filtering rules** (see section 7 for full detail). The generator function per model produces, in order:

1. `${ModelName}` interface (base)
2. `${ModelName}CreateInput` type
3. `${ModelName}UpdateInput` type
4. `${ModelName}WhereUniqueInput` type
5. `${ModelName}WhereInput` type
6. `${ModelName}Client` interface
7. `create${ModelName}Client` factory function type (as a `FunctionType` stored in a `constDeclaration`)

Signatures:

```typescript
import type * as DMMF from '@prisma/dmmf'
import { FileItem } from '@prisma/ts-builders'

export function generateModelDeclarations(
  model: DMMF.Model,
  enumNames: ReadonlySet<string>,
): FileItem[]
```

`enumNames` is used to distinguish enum fields from scalar fields when calling `scalarToTypeBuilder`.

**Return type for async methods.** Every method on `${ModelName}Client` returns `Promise<T>`. Build this with `namedType('Promise').addGenericArgument(T)`.

### 6.3 `src/generate-client.ts`

**Purpose.** Orchestrate building the full output `index.ts` string and write it to disk.

```typescript
import fs from 'node:fs/promises'
import path from 'node:path'

import type * as DMMF from '@prisma/dmmf'
import { file, moduleExport, stringify, typeDeclaration, ... } from '@prisma/ts-builders'

export async function generateRestClient(
  dmmf: DMMF.Document,
  outputDir: string,
): Promise<void>
```

Steps inside:
1. `await fs.mkdir(outputDir, { recursive: true })` to ensure the directory exists.
2. Build enum union type declarations from `dmmf.datamodel.enums`.
3. Collect `enumNames = new Set(dmmf.datamodel.enums.map(e => e.name))`.
4. For each model in `dmmf.datamodel.models`, call `generateModelDeclarations(model, enumNames)`.
5. Assemble the aggregate `PrismaRestClient` interface and `createPrismaRestClient` factory (see section 6.4).
6. Wrap all declarations with `moduleExport(...)` and feed into a `file()` builder, then `stringify(fileBuilder)`.
7. Write the result to `path.join(outputDir, 'index.ts')` with `fs.writeFile`.

### 6.4 Aggregate `PrismaRestClient`

After processing all models, produce:

```typescript
export interface PrismaRestClient {
  user: UserClient
  post: PostClient
  // ...one property per model, camelCase model name
}

export declare function createPrismaRestClient(baseUrl: string): PrismaRestClient
```

The `createPrismaRestClient` declaration is emitted as an `export declare function` (not implemented — consumers use it as a type-safe factory). Because `ts-builders` does not have a "declare function" builder directly, emit this as a raw string appended after `stringify(fileBuilder)`, OR use the `ConstDeclaration` builder with a `FunctionType` to produce `export const createPrismaRestClient: (baseUrl: string) => PrismaRestClient`. The latter is cleaner and uses existing builders.

Model name → property name: lowercase first character of PascalCase. `User` → `user`, `BlogPost` → `blogPost`.

### 6.5 `src/generator.ts`

```typescript
import type { GeneratorConfig, GeneratorManifest, GeneratorOptions } from '@prisma/generator'
import { parseEnvValue } from '@prisma/internals'

import { generateRestClient } from './generate-client'

export class RestClientGenerator {
  readonly name = 'rest-client-generator'

  getManifest(_config: GeneratorConfig): Promise<GeneratorManifest> {
    return Promise.resolve({
      prettyName: 'Prisma REST Client',
      defaultOutput: 'generated/rest-client',
    })
  }

  async generate(options: GeneratorOptions): Promise<void> {
    const outputDir = parseEnvValue(options.generator.output!)
    await generateRestClient(options.dmmf, outputDir)
  }
}
```

The `defaultOutput` value `'generated/rest-client'` is relative to the schema directory and is the fallback when the user omits `output` in the schema. No engine or engine version is required — the `requiresEngines` list stays absent/empty.

### 6.6 `src/index.ts`

This is the executable entry point. `generatorHandler` from `@prisma/generator-helper` reads JSON-RPC from stdin and writes results to stderr.

```typescript
import { generatorHandler } from '@prisma/generator-helper'

import { RestClientGenerator } from './generator'

const generator = new RestClientGenerator()

generatorHandler({
  onManifest(config) {
    return generator.getManifest(config)
  },
  async onGenerate(options) {
    await generator.generate(options)
  },
})
```

The `generatorHandler` call blocks the process — it installs readline listeners on `process.stdin` and calls `process.stdin.resume()`. The process stays alive until Prisma closes the pipe.

---

## 7. Field Filtering Rules

### 7.1 Base interface `${ModelName}`

Include: every field where `kind === 'scalar'` or `kind === 'enum'`.

Exclude: fields where `kind === 'object'` (relations) or `kind === 'unsupported'`.

Type resolution:
- `kind === 'scalar'`: call `scalarToTypeBuilder(field.type)`.
- `kind === 'enum'`: `namedType(field.type)` — the enum union type is declared in the same file.

Optionality: if `!field.isRequired`, call `.optional()` on the property.

List: if `field.isList`, wrap the type with `array(...)`.

### 7.2 `${ModelName}CreateInput`

Start from the same scalar+enum fields as the base interface. Exclude a field from `CreateInput` when ANY of these is true:

- `field.isReadOnly === true` — database-computed columns.
- `field.isGenerated === true` — auto-generated (e.g. `@default(autoincrement())`).
- `field.isUpdatedAt === true` — maintained by Prisma (`@updatedAt`).
- `field.isId && field.hasDefaultValue` — auto-generated IDs (uuid, cuid, autoincrement). The combination of `isId` and `hasDefaultValue` indicates the client never sets this.

Fields that remain: mark as optional when `!field.isRequired || field.hasDefaultValue`.

Rationale: if a field has a default value or is not required, the REST client may omit it.

### 7.3 `${ModelName}UpdateInput`

All scalar+enum fields, all optional. Partial update semantics — every field is `?: T | undefined`.

Exclude: same exclusions as `CreateInput` (read-only, generated, updatedAt). ID fields are excluded from `UpdateInput` entirely (you don't update the identifier via the body).

### 7.4 `${ModelName}WhereUniqueInput`

An object with exactly the unique identifier fields: those where `field.isId === true` OR `field.isUnique === true`.

All properties are optional at the TypeScript level (the caller picks one), though at runtime exactly one must be supplied. All types come from the scalar map.

### 7.5 `${ModelName}WhereInput`

All scalar+enum fields, all optional. Used for filtering in list operations. Same type resolution as the base interface, but every property is optional.

---

## 8. Enum Handling

From `dmmf.datamodel.enums` (type `DMMF.DatamodelEnum[]`), each enum becomes:

```typescript
export type UserRole = 'ADMIN' | 'USER' | 'GUEST'
```

Implementation with `ts-builders`:

```typescript
import { moduleExport, stringLiteral, typeDeclaration, unionType } from '@prisma/ts-builders'

function enumDeclaration(e: DMMF.DatamodelEnum): Export<TypeDeclaration> {
  const variants = e.values.map((v) => stringLiteral(v.name))
  const union = unionType(variants)
  return moduleExport(typeDeclaration(e.name, union))
}
```

`stringLiteral(v.name)` produces `'ADMIN'`, `'USER'`, etc. The `unionType([...])` constructor accepts an array and produces `'ADMIN' | 'USER' | 'GUEST'`.

---

## 9. URL/Endpoint Convention

Convert the PascalCase model name to a lowercase kebab-case plural path segment.

```
User      → /users
Post      → /posts
BlogPost  → /blog-posts
UserRole  → /user-roles
```

Algorithm:
1. Insert `-` before every uppercase letter that is immediately preceded by a lowercase letter or digit: `/([a-z0-9])([A-Z])/g` → `$1-$2`.
2. Convert to lowercase.
3. Append `s` for plural.

This is implemented in a `toRestPath(modelName: string): string` pure function in `model-file-generator.ts`.

The 5 CRUD methods on `${ModelName}Client` use these endpoints:

| Method | Signature | HTTP |
|---|---|---|
| `findMany` | `(where?: UserWhereInput) => Promise<User[]>` | `GET /users?where=...` |
| `findUnique` | `(where: UserWhereUniqueInput) => Promise<User \| null>` | `GET /users/:id` |
| `create` | `(data: UserCreateInput) => Promise<User>` | `POST /users` |
| `update` | `(where: UserWhereUniqueInput, data: UserUpdateInput) => Promise<User>` | `PATCH /users/:id` |
| `delete` | `(where: UserWhereUniqueInput) => Promise<User>` | `DELETE /users/:id` |

The `${ModelName}Client` interface is declared using `interfaceDeclaration(name).add(method(...))`. The `method(...)` builder takes the method name and uses `.setReturnType(...)` and `.addParameter(...)`. Return types are `Promise<T>` constructed as `namedType('Promise').addGenericArgument(namedType(ModelName))`, etc.

The factory function `create${ModelName}Client(baseUrl: string): ${ModelName}Client` has a concrete implementation using `fetch`. Since `ts-builders` operates on type-level constructs only, the factory body is emitted as a raw string appended to the file output after stringifying the structural declarations. This is the one place where raw string generation is used; everything else goes through the builder API.

The factory function body pattern:

```typescript
export function createUserClient(baseUrl: string): UserClient {
  const base = `${baseUrl}/users`
  return {
    async findMany(where) {
      const url = where ? `${base}?where=${encodeURIComponent(JSON.stringify(where))}` : base
      const res = await fetch(url)
      return res.json()
    },
    async findUnique(where) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`)
      if (res.status === 404) return null
      return res.json()
    },
    async create(data) {
      const res = await fetch(base, { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify(data) })
      return res.json()
    },
    async update(where, data) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`, { method: 'PATCH', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify(data) })
      return res.json()
    },
    async delete(where) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`, { method: 'DELETE' })
      return res.json()
    },
  }
}
```

**Implementation strategy for the factory body.** In `generate-client.ts`, after calling `stringify(fileBuilder)` for all the interface/type declarations, concatenate the factory function strings separately. Each factory string is built in `model-file-generator.ts` by a second function `generateModelFactory(model: DMMF.Model): string` that returns the raw TypeScript source. The two strings are joined with `\n\n`.

---

## 10. Sample Generated Output

For the example schema model:

```prisma
enum UserRole { ADMIN USER }

model User {
  id        Int      @id @default(autoincrement())
  name      String
  email     String   @unique
  role      UserRole
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())
  posts     Post[]
}
```

The generated `index.ts` content for the `User` model section:

```typescript
export type UserRole = 'ADMIN' | 'USER'

export interface User {
  id: number
  name: string
  email: string
  role: UserRole
  createdAt: string
}

export type UserCreateInput = {
  name: string
  email: string
  role: UserRole
  createdAt?: string
}

export type UserUpdateInput = {
  name?: string
  email?: string
  role?: UserRole
  createdAt?: string
}

export type UserWhereUniqueInput = {
  id?: number
  email?: string
}

export type UserWhereInput = {
  id?: number
  name?: string
  email?: string
  role?: UserRole
  createdAt?: string
}

export interface UserClient {
  findMany(where?: UserWhereInput): Promise<User[]>
  findUnique(where: UserWhereUniqueInput): Promise<User | null>
  create(data: UserCreateInput): Promise<User>
  update(where: UserWhereUniqueInput, data: UserUpdateInput): Promise<User>
  delete(where: UserWhereUniqueInput): Promise<User>
}

export function createUserClient(baseUrl: string): UserClient {
  const base = `${baseUrl}/users`
  return {
    async findMany(where) {
      const url = where ? `${base}?where=${encodeURIComponent(JSON.stringify(where))}` : base
      const res = await fetch(url)
      return res.json()
    },
    async findUnique(where) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`)
      if (res.status === 404) return null
      return res.json()
    },
    async create(data) {
      const res = await fetch(base, {
        method: 'POST',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify(data),
      })
      return res.json()
    },
    async update(where, data) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`, {
        method: 'PATCH',
        headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        body: JSON.stringify(data),
      })
      return res.json()
    },
    async delete(where) {
      const key = Object.values(where)[0]
      const res = await fetch(`${base}/${key}`, { method: 'DELETE' })
      return res.json()
    },
  }
}
```

Field-by-field analysis for `User`:

- `id: Int @id @default(autoincrement())`: excluded from `CreateInput` and `UpdateInput` (is ID + has default). Present in `WhereUniqueInput` and `WhereInput`. Type: `number`.
- `name: String`: required, no default → required in `CreateInput`, optional in `UpdateInput`.
- `email: String @unique`: required, no default, unique → required in `CreateInput`, appears in `WhereUniqueInput`.
- `role: UserRole`: enum field, required → `UserRole` type in all input types.
- `createdAt: DateTime @default(now())`: has default → optional in `CreateInput` (written as `createdAt?: string`). Excluded from `UpdateInput` only if marked `@updatedAt`; since it is not `@updatedAt` in this schema, it IS included as optional in `UpdateInput`.
- `posts: Post[]`: `kind === 'object'` → excluded from all interfaces.

---

## 11. Verification Steps

### Step 1: Build the package

```sh
cd packages/rest-client-generator
pnpm build
```

Expected: `dist/index.js`, `dist/index.mjs`, `dist/index.d.ts`, `dist/index.d.mts` all created.

### Step 2: Run unit tests

```sh
cd packages/rest-client-generator
pnpm test
```

Tests in `src/__tests__/type-map.test.ts` should cover every DMMF scalar string and verify the output `TypeBuilder` name via `stringify`. Tests in `src/__tests__/model-file-generator.test.ts` should use a hand-crafted `DMMF.Model` object and verify the full generated output with `toMatchInlineSnapshot`.

### Step 3: Type-check with the monorepo tsconfig

```sh
# from repo root
pnpm tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.build.bundle.json
```

The new path alias entry means `@prisma/rest-client-generator` resolves to `./packages/rest-client-generator/src` for any other package that imports it.

### Step 4: End-to-end integration test

Create a minimal test fixture in `sandbox/` (already gitignored per the root `.gitignore` pattern):

```
sandbox/rest-test/
  schema.prisma
```

`schema.prisma`:
```prisma
generator restClient {
  provider = "node dist/index.js"
  output   = "./generated"
}

datasource db {
  provider = "sqlite"
  url      = "file:./dev.db"
}

model User {
  id    Int    @id @default(autoincrement())
  name  String
  email String @unique
}
```

Run:
```sh
cd sandbox/rest-test
npx prisma generate
```

Expected: `sandbox/rest-test/generated/index.ts` is created with correct content.

Inspect:
```sh
cat sandbox/rest-test/generated/index.ts
```

### Step 5: Type-check the generated file

```sh
cd sandbox/rest-test
npx tsc --noEmit --strict generated/index.ts --target ES2022 --lib ES2022,DOM --moduleResolution bundler --module preserve
```

No errors expected.

### Step 6: Turbo build integration

From the repo root:
```sh
turbo build --filter=@prisma/rest-client-generator
```

Turbo reads `turbo.json`, sees the `build` task, and respects `dependsOn: ["^build"]`. The package's dependencies (`@prisma/dmmf`, `@prisma/generator`, `@prisma/generator-helper`, `@prisma/internals`, `@prisma/ts-builders`) must build first.

---

## 12. Architecture Notes and Trade-offs

**Why `generatorHandler` and not the `Generator` interface directly.** `client-generator-js` and `client-generator-ts` implement the `Generator` interface from `@prisma/generator` and are invoked programmatically through the `client-generator-registry`. An external generator (invoked via `provider = "node dist/index.js"`) must use the JSON-RPC protocol, which `generatorHandler` from `@prisma/generator-helper` implements. This package is designed as a standalone executable generator, not an internal programmatic one, so `generatorHandler` is the right primitive.

**Why raw string for factory bodies.** The `ts-builders` library produces typed declaration trees (interfaces, type aliases, methods). It has no `FunctionDeclaration` builder with a body. Emitting the factory function as a template-literal string is the pragmatic and convention-consistent approach — the declaration half (interface and types) uses builders, the implementation half uses raw strings. Both are joined in the final output with a blank line separator.

**Why `File` builder for declarations.** The `File` builder from `@prisma/ts-builders` handles proper newline spacing between declarations automatically (`\n` between items). Using it for the structural declarations avoids manual newline management.

**BigInt / Decimal as `string`.** REST/JSON cannot represent `BigInt` or `Decimal` natively. Serialising as `string` is the safest cross-platform convention. If callers need numeric precision, they parse the string themselves.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/packages/generator-helper/src/generatorHandler.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/packages/ts-builders/src/index.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/packages/dmmf/src/dmmf.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/helpers/compile/configs.ts`
- `/private/var/folders/vq/9q38rw0x2455zr_whqcrjbhr0000gn/T/planmode-bench-wt-run-1775491877978-p4yptr/tsconfig.build.bundle.json`
